content.full_text:
FE: 
TELEPHONE CONVERSATION ~HTH liSSEI'·IBLYMili\i FRl-li'!K MURPHY 
(3/22/76) (3/24/76) 
Frank Hurphy, Assemblyman from Monterey I 
called on 
3/24/76 with the following question. 
He was retained by an-
ot.her legis la-tor as an attorney to represent the legislator 
in the investigation being conducted by the FPPC into the 
CAL-PAC honorarium question. 
Hr. H. wanted to know whether 
or not it was permissible for the legislator to pay his legal 
fee out of the legislator's campaign funds. 
I responded on 3/26/76 that there was no prohibition 
against using campaign funds for this purpose; hO'dever 1 
the legislators' committee vlOuld have to report this as an 
expendi ture and Assemblyman Murphy \'lOuld have to report thi s 
as income if his pro rata share of the fees exceed $1,000. 
Although I did not speak to Assemblyman M. about this point, 
I looked into the question of whether or not the legislator 
would have to report the expenditure by his committee as 
income, 
under the theory that this vIas a gift! to him. 
I 
revie\ved the Willie Brown opinion and the Hayer Hayes opinion 
and talked with Ken Finney and Bob Stern and concluded that 
this expendi ture by the committee 'i:lOUld come under the broad 
heading of political expenditures and 'tvould no·t be for the 
personal business of a legislator. 
Therefore, it v70uld not 
be a gift and would not have to be reported as income. 
HJB:pvp 
4/29/76

sections.question: Whether or not it was permissible for the legislator to pay his legal fee out of the legislator's campaign funds.
sections.conclusion: There was no prohibition against using campaign funds for this purpose; however, the legislators' committee would have to report this as an expenditure and Assemblyman Murphy would have to report this as income if his pro rata share of the fees exceed $1,000. The expenditure by the committee would come under the broad heading of political expenditures and would not be for the personal business of a legislator. Therefore, it would not be a gift and would not have to be reported as income.
sections.question_synthetic: A legislator retained another legislator as his attorney to represent him in an FPPC investigation into CAL-PAC honorariums. The question is whether the legislator may pay the legal fees from his campaign funds.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Campaign funds may be used to pay legal fees, with the committee reporting it as an expenditure. The attorney must report income if his pro rata share exceeds $1,000, but the legislator need not report the committee's expenditure as income since it constitutes a political expenditure rather than a personal gift.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document is a telephone conversation memo dated 3/22/76-4/29/76. No formal Q/C section headers present; question and conclusion extracted from narrative text. Some reference to related opinions (Willie Brown, Mayer Hayes) noted but not included in response text.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether or not it was permissible for the legislator to pay his legal fee out of the legislator's campaign funds.

CONCLUSION: There was no prohibition against using campaign funds for this purpose; however, the legislators' committee would have to report this as an expenditure and Assemblyman Murphy would have to report this as income if his pro rata share of the fees exceed $1,000. The expenditure by the committee would come under the broad heading of political expenditures and would not be for the personal business of a legislator. Therefore, it would not be a gift and would not have to be reported as income.

He was retained by an- ot.her legis la-tor as an attorney to represent the legislator in the investigation being conducted by the FPPC into the CAL-PAC honorarium question. Hr. H. wanted to know whether or not it was permissible for the legislator to pay his legal fee out of the legislator's campaign funds. I responded on 3/26/76 that there was no prohibition against using campaign funds for this purpose; hO'dever 1 the legislators' committee vlOuld have to report this as an expendi ture and Assemblyman Murphy \'lOuld have to report thi s as income if his pro rata share of the fees exceed $1,000. Although I did not speak to Assemblyman M. about this point, I looked into the question of whether or not the legislator would have to report the expenditure by his committee as income, under the theory that this vIas a gift! to him. I revie\ved the Willie Brown opinion and the Hayer Hayes opinion and talked with Ken Finney and Bob Stern and concluded that this expendi ture by the committee 'i:lOUld come under the broad heading of political expenditures and 'tvould no·t be for the personal business of a legislator. Therefore, it v70uld not be a gift and would not have to be reported as income. HJB:pvp 4/29/76
embedding.summary: FPPC advised that a legislator may use campaign funds to pay legal fees for representation in an FPPC investigation, provided proper reporting requirements are met. The expenditure is treated as a political expense and not a taxable gift to the legislator.
